title: I Work a Manual Labor Job at Lowes During the Day and Build for Fortune 500 Company
excerpt: Retail by day builder by night.
body:
My alarm goes off at 6am.



I throw on my work clothes and head into my day job at a home improvement store where I do manual labor. Moving heavy product, managing inventory, keeping operations running before the rest of the world is even awake.



By most measures it looks like a pretty ordinary morning.



But by the time I clock out I might have a response waiting from a Fortune 500 company about a corporate strategy project. Or a meeting scheduled with a nonprofit newsroom to demo a web application I built. Or a kickoff call with one of the biggest talent agencies in the world.



That's my life right now. And I wouldn't change it.



How It Started



In early May 2026 I came across Parker Dewey.



I wasn't looking for a side hustle. I wasn't trying to build a personal brand. I was just looking for a way to get real experience that matched what I was actually capable of without waiting for someone to give me a traditional opportunity.



Parker Dewey runs micro internships. Short paid project based engagements with real companies. No full time commitment. No months long application process. You apply, you get selected, you deliver, you move on.



I applied. I got selected. I delivered.



That first project was for Reynolds American Inc., a subsidiary of British American Tobacco. I built a corporate strategy deliverable that went directly to division leadership. Nobody told me exactly how to do it. I just figured out what the job required and delivered it.



The Moment That Changed How I Saw Myself



When Laura at United Talent Agency reviewed applicants for their micro internship project she told me I came out in the top 1% of everyone she evaluated.



She based that on my resume and LinkedIn alone.



I am still not sure that has fully sunk in.



UTA is one of the biggest talent agencies in the world. Their entire business is identifying the right people. Their data pointed to me.



Not because of where I went to school. Not because of who I knew. Because of what I had actually built and the credentials I had actually earned.



That was the moment I stopped questioning whether I belonged in those rooms.



What the Days Actually Look Like



I clock in before most people have had their first cup of coffee.



The morning is physical. Moving product, managing inventory, keeping things running. The work is straightforward. You do it or you don't. There is something grounding about that honestly.



Then I clock out and the other work begins.



Applications to corporate projects. Building demos. Writing code. Researching companies. Drafting deliverables. Publishing articles. Running MCC Solutions LLC, my licensed financial services firm based in Cranford NJ.



Last night I published my first Medium article, submitted five corporate project applications across private equity, Fortune 500 agriculture, federal government, and global infrastructure, rebuilt my professional website, and prepped a full stack web application demo for a client meeting happening Thursday.



All of that after a full day of manual labor.



What I Actually Built



Since May 2026 here is what I have been working on:



A corporate strategy deliverable for Reynolds American that went directly to division leadership.



A talent pipeline tool for United Talent Agency that pulls YouTube creator data to identify emerging talent before the big agencies get there first.



A full stack obituary aggregation platform for a Richmond nonprofit newsroom complete with a Python scraper, a database, an admin editorial approval portal, and a public facing search interface.



Applications pending with Xylem, the USDA, LLR Partners, and ADM Global Business Services.



None of this required a master's degree. None of it required a prestigious internship. None of it required anyone's permission.



It required showing up. Building things. Delivering without being asked twice.



What I Want You to Take From This



I am not writing this to impress anyone.



I am writing this because I spent a long time thinking the gap between where I was and where I wanted to be was too wide to cross without the right credential, the right school, the right connection, or the right moment.



It is not.



The gap closes one project at a time. One article at a time. One early morning and one late night at a time.



Most people wait for perfect conditions to start building something.



I started in May with a Parker Dewey application and a LinkedIn profile. Two months later I have active projects with a Big 4 talent agency, a Fortune 500 company, and the federal government.



The manual labor job pays the bills while I build something that eventually makes it optional.



You do not need permission to start.



You just need to start.



My alarm goes off at 6am every morning.



What time does yours go off?





Matthew Coleman is the owner of MCC Solutions LLC, a licensed financial services firm in Cranford NJ. He is currently completing a corporate project for United Talent Agency through Parker Dewey while building toward full time entrepreneurship.
